title: On Hölder continuous globally dissipative Euler flows
speaker: Hyunju Kwon
abstract: In the theory of turbulence, a famous conjecture of Onsager asserts that the threshold Hölder regularity for the total kinetic energy conservation of (spatially periodic) Euler flows is 1/3. In particular, there are Hölder continuous Euler flows with Hölder exponent less than 1/3 exhibiting strict energy dissipation, as proved recently by Isett. In light of these developments, I'll discuss Hölder continuous Euler flows which not only have energy dissipation but also satisfy a local energy inequality. This is joint work with Camillo De Lellis.

proof_stages.label: Euler weak solutions and Onsager energy threshold
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces incompressible Euler as the zero-viscosity limit of Navier-Stokes, formulates weak solutions and pressure recovery, derives the formal kinetic-energy balance, and recalls Onsager's Hölder one-third conservation/dissipation threshold.

proof_stages.label: Local energy inequality and strengthened dissipative problem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines the local energy inequality, explains it as a time-oriented admissibility condition related to vanishing-viscosity Navier-Stokes limits, and states the strengthened Onsager-type construction problem together with the current Hölder threshold result, improving earlier constructions and yielding strict local dissipation.

proof_stages.label: Coarse-graining commutator obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The positive Onsager argument is revisited by averaging the equation, producing a Reynolds-type commutator whose estimate vanishes only above exponent one third and whose possible non-vanishing motivates high-frequency perturbations.

proof_stages.label: Relaxed convex-integration iteration
proof_stages.summary: A nonlinear convex-integration iteration is set up in which approximate solutions solve a relaxed Euler/local-energy system with a Reynolds stress and an unresolved current, and the next perturbation must be highly oscillatory, divergence-free, and cancel the previous errors at low frequency.

proof_stages.label: Mikado cancellation of Reynolds stress
proof_stages.summary: Using the geometric lemma, the speaker first explains why plane waves create unwanted cross-interactions and then replaces them by disjointly supported Mikado flows whose low-frequency self-interactions cancel the Reynolds stress.

proof_stages.label: Decoupled cancellation of the local-energy current
proof_stages.summary: A vector-valued geometric decomposition is introduced so the low-frequency cubic part of the perturbation cancels the unresolved current, while an added Mikado family with suitable mean-square and zero cubic-mean conditions preserves the Reynolds-stress cancellation.

proof_stages.label: Transported Mikados, time slots, and the one-seventh loss
proof_stages.summary: To minimize the dominant transport error, Mikado flows are advected by the previous velocity, restarted on short time intervals, and spatially discretized with shifted supports to avoid interference; the spatial discretization and cutoff costs are identified as the source of the achieved exponent below one seventh and of the added difficulty from the local energy inequality.

examples.name: No-flux nonperiodic domains for the energy balance
examples.note: The speaker notes that the formal kinetic-energy conservation computation also works on the whole space or on bounded domains with boundary conditions killing flux, illustrating why the torus is a simplifying choice rather than part of the phenomenon.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker says the same energy-balance computation would hold on whole or bounded domains with vanishing boundary flux, but does not analyze such a domain separately

passes.reason: no-flux whole or bounded domains are a standard setting where the boundary term vanishes, not a sporadic or limiting object and not used to refute a claim

examples.name: Fractional Sobolev/Besov alternatives to Hölder regularity
examples.note: An audience question raises fractional Hilbert/Sobolev and Besov-type spaces as finer regularity scales around the Onsager threshold, serving as a side illustration of how the one-third exponent can be formulated beyond Hölder spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: fractional Sobolev/Besov/Hilbert alternatives are discussed in the Q&A as possible regularity spaces, without computing or proving anything in a specific space

passes.reason: fractional Sobolev and Besov spaces are systematic regularity scales offered as alternatives to Hölder spaces rather than as failures of a statement

examples.name: Time reversal of a dissipative Euler flow
examples.note: The time-reversed version of a dissipative weak Euler solution would have increasing kinetic energy, illustrating why the local energy inequality imposes a physically meaningful direction of time.
examples.engagement_reason: time reversal is invoked to note that reversing a dissipative Euler flow would give energy increase and be ruled out by the local energy inequality, with no concrete construction worked through

passes.reason: the time-reversed dissipative Euler flow is an ordinary weak Euler flow structurally, but is invoked as the unphysical energy-increasing scenario ruled out by the local energy inequality
The time-reversed dissipative Euler flow is used as an unphysical energy-increasing scenario that the local energy inequality is meant to rule out.

examples.name: Littlewood-Paley cutoff as averaging operator
examples.note: Using a Littlewood-Paley projection makes the coarse-grained commutator concrete and visibly reveals the high-frequency self-interaction mechanism that motivates the convex-integration ansatz.
examples.engagement_reason: the Littlewood-Paley cutoff is applied to the Euler equation, producing an averaged equation, commutator error, tested energy identity, and an estimate leading to conservation above one third

passes.reason: a Littlewood-Paley low-frequency cutoff is a standard Fourier-analytic smoothing/projection tool used in the conservation argument, not as an adversarial example
